summary: CPU-only inference latency across the kubelm tier ladder
rationale: kubelm is a CPU-only model family spanning a resource spectrum (smallest local box -> dev machine); per-tier CPU latency is a first-class metric (PROJECT.md commitment #7, <5s/step target). Prior benches ran GPU-offloaded; this is the first CPU-only characterization.

host.note: M1 Max has ~400GB/s unified memory bandwidth + Accelerate BLAS; an UPPER bound. Commodity/constrained CPUs (the real ultra-edge target) will be several x slower. Relative ordering across tiers holds.

tool: llama-bench -ngl 0 -p 2048 -n 128 -r 3 (CPU-only; validated vs -ngl 99: 2B tg 48.6 CPU vs 124.5 Metal, 2.6x => -ngl 0 is genuine CPU)

real_scenario_runs.note: Full K8sGPT-MCP investigation (not synthetic), llama-server -ngl 0, model latency only (excludes tool/cluster time).
real_scenario_runs.outcome: complete, rubric PASSED, schema 3/3, refcalls passed (correct conclusion)

real_linux_envelope_2core.note: THE deployment-faithful measurement. Real x86_64 Linux container (RunPod CPU pod, 2 vCPU / 4GB, runpod-ubuntu), llama.cpp built from source, CPU backend (no Accelerate), -ngl 0, -t 2. M1+Accelerate is an optimistic ceiling; kind-on-Mac-Docker-VM was a PATHOLOGICAL floor (2B tg 5 t/s due to VM-throttled bandwidth) and is discarded.
findings: A 2-core / 4GB node runs EVERY tier with headroom: true serving footprint at -c16384 is 0.8B ~892MB, 2B ~1628MB (--no-mmap RSS). RAM is NOT the binding constraint at these model sizes -- CPU/latency is. The prior 4GB/8GB 'RAM floors' were far too conservative (training-era / guesses, not serving). The hybrid linear-attention KV cache is compact.
Real-Linux 2-core per-step latency: 0.8B ~16s typical / ~32s heavy; 2B ~29s / ~55s. Full investigation (4-7 steps): 0.8B ~1-2 min, 2B ~2-4 min -- usable SRE diagnostic on a tiny node.

HOST VARIANCE IS ~10x AND IS THE DOMINANT CAVEAT: per-step latency depends far more on the CPU host than on the tier. The per-tier latencies above are a DEDICATED-vCPU reference, NOT a guarantee. Two real RunPod CPU pods (same '2 vCPU' flavor) differed ~7-10x: a fast modern x86 host (2B pp 88 / tg 27 t/s) vs a throttled/oversubscribed host (1.5B pp ~12 / tg ~6 t/s). Oversubscribed, burstable, or contended K8s nodes degrade sharply. RAM (host-independent) is unaffected.

environment_variance.note: Same workload (Qwen3.5/2.5 Q4_K_M, -ngl 0, ~2 threads), wildly different throughput by environment. Size from the dedicated-vCPU reference; treat anything oversubscribed/burstable as much slower.

points.verdict: optimistic ceiling -- do NOT ship from this

recommended_pod_spec.ultra-edge_0.8B: requests cpu 2 / mem 1.5Gi, limits cpu 4 / mem 2Gi (threads=cpu limit)
recommended_pod_spec.edge+_2B: requests cpu 2 / mem 2.5Gi, limits cpu 4 / mem 3Gi
recommended_pod_spec.note: mem = serving footprint + compute headroom for a ~4K-token prompt (host-independent, reliable). cpu/latency is host-dependent (~10x swing) -- prefer DEDICATED (guaranteed) cores; on burstable/oversubscribed nodes expect multiples of the reference latency. 1.5B latency is ESTIMATED (its real-Linux run landed on a throttled host, non-comparable).

findings: 0.8B is decisively the fastest CPU tier (~1.6x the 2B's prompt-processing, best token-gen) and the only tier close to the <5s/step target -> validates the ultra-edge rung.

Architectural: Qwen3.5-2B processes prompts FASTER than Qwen2.5-1.5B despite more params (hybrid linear-attention is cheaper on large tool-schema prompts). The ladder is not strictly monotonic in params for prompt-processing.
No tier achieves <5s on a COLD step even on M1 Max; on weaker CPUs the gap widens -> the latency commitment is real pressure and the ultra-edge tier's reason to exist.

Real-scenario proof: 0.8B on 2 cores completes a correct K8sGPT investigation in ~76s (5 steps, ~15s/step) vs 2B on 6 cores at ~191s (4 steps, ~48s/step). Per-step cost is dominated by prompt processing (heaviest step = 52s on a 3104-token prompt), so latency scales with accumulated tool-output size, not step count.

DONE: real-Linux 2-core CPU+RAM envelope measured (see real_linux_envelope_2core). Key correction: RAM floors were ~5x too high; both tiers fit a 4GB node. Higher-core points scale ~linearly with cores per the bare-metal curve.
